does: This paper proposes a novel alignment method for LLM-based agents that uses explicitly designed reward functions encoding human values for reinforcement learning-based fine-tuning, rather than relying on implicit human preference data. The approach applies intrinsic rewards grounded in philosophical frameworks—specifically deontological ethics—to guide agent behavior toward moral alignment. The work demonstrates this methodology by evaluating LLM agents on decision-making tasks where moral values are transparently encoded into reward structures, offering a more interpretable alternative to black-box preference learning methods.
connects: This research builds on the growing literature of AI alignment and value alignment, extending beyond standard approaches like RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) and DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) that rely on opaque human preference comparisons. It connects to work on interpretable AI, mechanistic transparency in language models, and the broader challenge of aligning increasingly capable autonomous agents with human values. The paper engages with philosophical frameworks for ethics—drawing on deontological theory—responding to calls for more principled, values-based approaches to alignment rather than purely data-driven preference matching.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing more interpretable and philosophically grounded alignment methods that could scale to increasingly general autonomous agents. By making moral values explicit and verifiable in reward design, the approach enables better auditing and explanation of agent behavior, supporting deployment in high-stakes domains where transparency is critical. It points toward hybrid alignment frameworks that combine formal ethical theories with learned behaviors, and suggests future work applying other philosophical frameworks (consequentialism, virtue ethics) to agent fine-tuning. This foundation could enable safer development of multi-objective agents that balance competing values transparently.
